title: The missing link
speaker: Vidit Nanda
abstract: Links of strata in singular spaces are fundamental invariants which govern the topology of small neighbourhoods around points in those strata. This talk will focus on inferring links of strata from incomplete information in three completely different contexts. In each case, there are exciting consequences of learning the structure of such links.
tags: Workshop on Topology: Identifying Order in Complex Systems

proof_stages.label: Whitney-stratified spaces as the replacement for manifold transitivity
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from the transitive action of diffeomorphisms on connected manifolds, then motivates filtered spaces with smooth strata and Whitney condition B as the singular-space setting where transitivity along strata is recovered.

proof_stages.label: Normal slices, links, and conical neighborhoods
proof_stages.summary: For a stratum in a Whitney stratified space, a transverse normal slice and a small boundary sphere define the link, which is independent of the point and choices and gives the local model R^i times the cone on the link.

proof_stages.label: Local persistent links for maritime trajectory graphs
proof_stages.summary: The first application uses circle-crossing data around grid points and zero-dimensional persistent homology to estimate local degree, thereby learning an embedded shipping-lane graph and scoring new trajectories by distance from it.

proof_stages.label: Intersection-theoretic multiplicity as an algebraic target
proof_stages.summary: The second story sets up projective varieties, birational equivalence, Chow-type intersection classes, Segre classes, Hilbert-Samuel multiplicity, and degree as the algebraic quantities the speaker wants to compute without heavy elimination.

proof_stages.label: Degree formulas and reduction to point-on-curve multiplicity
proof_stages.summary: A degree formula for e_XY is specialized to the case of a point p in a variety, then hyperplane-section invariance reduces the general multiplicity problem to computing e_pC for a point on a complex curve.

proof_stages.label: Thom isotopy and Euler-characteristic localization
proof_stages.summary: Using Thom's first isotopy lemma for a nondegenerate projection, the difference between the cardinalities of nearby and special hyperplane sections is localized near p and rewritten as an Euler characteristic calculation.

proof_stages.label: Complex-link formula and equation-free computation
proof_stages.summary: The localized space is identified with the complex link, yielding the theorem that the desired Hilbert-Samuel multiplicity is the Euler characteristic of a complex link and can in principle be inferred from sampled points rather than defining equations.
